references.title: Towards Accurate and Fair Prediction of College Success: Evaluating Different Sources of Student Data

extracted.Target: Final course grade above class median vs below class median
extracted.Courses: 10 fully online introductory STEM courses at a large public U.S. university
extracted.Data sources: SIS data, LMS logs, Survey Data
extracted.Features: [female, transfer, low income, first-generation, URM, SAT total score, high school GPA, total clicks and total time in the first half of the course, plus category-specific clicks/time (portal, tasks, content, communication, performance, miscellaneous), effort regulation, time management, environment management, self-efficacy]
extracted.Moment of Prediction: First half of course / first 5 weeks
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Students with missing values on more than 25% of features were dropped. Continuous numerical variables were standardized by centering to the median and scaling by the IQR. Remaining missing values were handled with multivariate imputation. Click features were restricted to the first half of the course to support early identification.
extracted.Assessment Strategy: Course-level leave-one-group-out cross-validation across the 10 courses
extracted.Comments: Institutional and LMS data both had useful predictive value, and combining them improved performance. Survey data alone had weak predictive utility. In fairness terms, institutional data tended to underpredict historically disadvantaged students, while LMS data sometimes overpredicted some groups.
extracted.Limitations: Only 10 online STEM courses at one institution. Click features were deliberately coarse and not course-specific. Survey features covered only a limited set of self-regulated learning constructs. The study evaluates fairness but does not test bias-mitigation strategies.

extracted.Target: Average GPA in the following academic year above class median vs below class median
extracted.Courses: 10 fully online introductory STEM courses at a large public U.S. university
extracted.Data sources: SIS data, LMS logs, Survey Data
extracted.Features: [female, transfer, low income, first-generation, URM, SAT total score, high school GPA, total clicks and total time in the first half of the course, plus category-specific clicks/time (portal, tasks, content, communication, performance, miscellaneous), effort regulation, time management, environment management, self-efficacy]
extracted.Moment of Prediction: First half of course, predicting following-year GPA
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Students with missing values on more than 25% of features were dropped. Continuous numerical variables were standardized by centering to the median and scaling by the IQR. Remaining missing values were handled with multivariate imputation. Click features were computed from the first half of the course, while the target was long-term GPA in the following academic year.
extracted.Assessment Strategy: Course-level leave-one-group-out cross-validation across the 10 courses. For each feature-set/outcome combination, the classifier with the highest F-score was retained. Evaluation considered Accuracy, FPR, and FNR, with subgroup fairness analysis via disparity ratios and one-sided two-proportion z-tests.
extracted.Comments: LMS and institutional data both predicted long-term success reasonably well, and their combination performed best. Survey data again showed limited added value. Fairness analysis showed persistent underestimation of disadvantaged groups when institutional data were used, and combining data sources did not fully remove bias.
extracted.Limitations: Only 10 online STEM courses at one institution. Long-term success is operationalized relative to classmates via median split rather than raw GPA. Fairness was analyzed at the data-source level, but no debiasing intervention was tested.

annotations.text: Learning analytics predictive models are not always fair to all student subgroups. To show this, authors compare the effectiveness of features extracted from 3 different data sources: SIS, LMS and Survey. The analysis is made in a 2-fold manner:

Authors found that LMS and Institutional data are, indeed, predictive of student performance and that performance is increased when both sources are combined. However, the authors also identified that under-represented groups tend to have their performance be under-estimated, meaning that these students may, under the current regimen, be more likely to slip through the cracks of a warning system.
